# Summary of "Shared Values for a Troubled World: Conversations with Men and Women of Conscience"

### Summary of

# *Shared Values for a Troubled World: Conversations with Men and Women of Conscience*

## By Rushworth M. Kidder

### Summary written by Conflict Research Consortium Staff

**Citation:** Rushworth M. Kidder. *Shared Values for a Troubled World: Conversations with Men and Women of Conscience*. San Francisco: Jossey-Bass Publishers, 1994, 332 pp.

*Shared Values for a Troubled World: Conversations with Men and Women of Conscience* attempts to identify a core of globally shared ethical values. The author interviews twenty-four notable thinkers of diverse interests and cultural backgrounds, asking each to describe their fundamental moral principles. Drawing on these interviews, Kidder describes a core of common ethical values.

*Shared Values for a Troubled World: Conversations with Men and Women of Conscience* will be of interest to those who seek an ethical "common ground" from which to address issues of global significance. This work is divided into seven chapters. The first chapter argues that we need a globally shared code of ethics to "help create the moral conditions for a sustainable twenty-first century." Chapters two through six present the interviews, grouped by topic. In the final chapter the author distills an eight-point code of ethical values from these interviews.

Chapter two focuses on religion, philanthropy and charity. This chapter includes interviews with Reuban Snake, Native-American tribal chief; James A. Joseph, executive with a national philanthropic foundation; Shojun Bando, Buddhist monk; Le Ly Hayslip, Vietnamese writer and activist; and Father Bernard Przewozny, administrator of an international environmental prize.

Chapter three considers the ethical values associated with education and youth, interviewing Graca Machel, former first lady of Mozambique; Derek Bok, former Harvard University president; Dame Whina Cooper, New Zealand Maori activist; A. H. Halsey, Oxford don; and Jill Ker Conway, Australian author and former Smith College president.

Chapter four, Entrepreneurs and the Economy, concentrates on ethics in business. Interviewed are: Muhammad Yunus, a Bangladeshi banker; Kenneth Boulding, British economist and author; James K. Baker, American CEO; Newton Minnow, former Federal Communications Commission chairman; John W. Gardner, founder of the citizen lobbying organization Common Cause.

Chapter five, discusses values in literature and journalism. The writers interviewed include Nien Cheng, Chinese author; Varindra Tarzie Vittachi, Sri Lankan journalist and former United Nations official; Astrid Lindgren, Swedish author; Sergio Munoz, Hispanic columnist and editor; and Katharine Whitehorn, British columnist.

Chapter six focuses on issues of political leadership, government and politics. Individuals interviewed include Oscar Arias, Nobel Laureate, Salim El Hoss, former Prime Minister of Lebanon; Jeane Kirkpatrick, former U. N. ambassador; Federico Mayor, director-general of UNESCO.

The final chapter discusses eight core values which constitute a global code of ethics: love, truthfulness, fairness, freedom, unity, tolerance, responsibility, and respect for life.

--- SOURCE 2: Global Ethics Frameworks → Term ---
URL: https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/global-ethics-frameworks/

SUMMARY:
Global Ethics Frameworks represent a shared understanding of responsible global citizenship. ... shared values, participatory decision-making

FULL CONTENT:
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Global Ethics Frameworks](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/global-ethics-frameworks-term-image.webp)

# Global Ethics Frameworks

![An abstract sculpture combines earthy textures with sleek forms, demonstrating eco conscious living. This artwork embodies the harmony sought in environmental art and sustainable design principles. The image provokes thoughtful consideration regarding conscious choices, aiming for a green lifestyle that emphasizes mindful consumption.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-sustainable-art-installation-exploring-the-interconnectedness-of.jpg "Abstract Sustainable Art Installation Exploring the Interconnectedness of Elements in a Green Lifestyle Philosophy")

## Fundamentals

Imagine for a moment that the world is a shared home, not just a collection of countries but a single, interconnected space where everyone resides. In this home, decisions ripple outwards, touching lives far beyond our immediate view. **Global Ethics Frameworks** are essentially the agreed-upon guidelines for living well together in this global home. They are not rigid laws handed down from above, but rather evolving understandings about how to behave responsibly on a planetary scale.

Think about your own life. You likely have personal ethics – beliefs about what’s right and wrong that guide your choices. You might value honesty, kindness, or fairness. Now, broaden that personal view to encompass the entire planet.

What does fairness look like when it comes to sharing resources, protecting the environment, or ensuring everyone has a chance at a decent life? These frameworks begin to answer those big questions. They recognize that actions taken in one corner of the world can have profound consequences elsewhere, from [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  change affecting distant communities to economic policies impacting global markets. They push us to consider not just what we *can* do, but what we *should* do, in a world where our lives are increasingly intertwined.

![Against a backdrop of a desolate desert, a woman presents minimalist sustainable fashion, dressed in flowing pale pink ethical clothing crafted from natural fibers. Her choice highlights conscious consumerism and durable garments reflecting a timeless style, promoting green living and reduced waste. The stark landscape emphasizes the need for responsible choices and earth-friendly fashion.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/minimalist-sustainable-fashion-in-arid-landscape-showcasing-ethical-clothing.jpg "Minimalist Sustainable Fashion in Arid Landscape Showcasing Ethical Clothing and Eco Friendly Materials for Green Lifestyle")

### Understanding the Building Blocks

To truly grasp [Global Ethics Meaning → Shared ethical principles guiding actions in our interconnected world, for a just and sustainable future.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/global-ethics/)  Frameworks, it’s helpful to break them down into simpler ideas. They are built upon core principles that many people, across different cultures and backgrounds, find to be important. These principles act as a foundation for ethical decision-making on a global scale.

Imagine a simple [scenario Meaning → A scenario is a potential future outcome based on a coherent set of assumptions, a key tool for analysis on the Prism domain.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  → you are sharing a pizza with friends. [Ethical frameworks Meaning → Ethical frameworks, within the realm of sustainability, represent structured systems of moral principles and values designed to guide decision-making and actions.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/ethical-frameworks/)  at a personal level might guide you to ensure everyone gets a fair slice (justice). Thinking about the future, you might consider not wasting any pizza (sustainability). Recognizing everyone’s hunger is valid reflects a basic human right to sustenance.

And if you ordered too much pizza, taking responsibility to either eat the leftovers or offer them to someone else shows personal accountability. Global Ethics Frameworks extend this kind of everyday ethical thinking to the grand scale of global interactions.

![A song sparrow with intricate feather details perches on a weathered wooden post, exemplifying natural habitat within an accessible view. This image promotes biodiversity and habitat conservation, key tenets of a sustainable life. It encourages green living and environmental awareness, showcasing urban wildlife as a vital part of our ecological balance.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/observing-intricate-feather-details-of-song-sparrow-perched-on-weathered-wood.jpg "Observing Intricate Feather Details of Song Sparrow Perched on Weathered Wood Embodying Natural Habitat Conservation and Sustainable Lifestyle Principles")

### Why Should You Care?

It might feel like these frameworks are something for politicians or international organizations to worry about, but they touch your life in very real ways. Consider the clothes you wear, the food you eat, or the technology you use daily. Many of these things are produced and sourced globally. Global Ethics Frameworks encourage us to ask questions about the conditions under which these products are made.

Are workers treated fairly? Is the environment being protected in the process? Your consumer choices, informed by ethical considerations, can contribute to a more just and sustainable world. Understanding these frameworks can shift your perspective, making you a more informed and conscientious global citizen.

Furthermore, understanding these principles helps in navigating complex global issues. From understanding news about international conflicts to engaging in discussions about climate policy, a basic grasp of global ethics provides a lens through which to analyze situations and form informed opinions. It’s about moving beyond a purely self-centered view and recognizing our place within a larger web of interconnected lives and systems. It’s about cultivating a sense of global citizenship, where your ethical compass extends beyond your immediate community to encompass the well-being of people and the planet as a whole.

![This evocative sculpture highlights eco-conscious living by combining raw elements into a display advocating sustainable life. Balanced stones, a reclaimed wood piece and soft fluid clouds represent nature's delicate yet resilient ecological harmony. Secured by delicate wire this symbolic arrangement communicates a core message focusing on environmental stewardship promoting mindful sustainable choices and encourages sustainable consumer behavior reinforcing responsible resource management in a simple mindful art piece for green living.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-sculpture-balance-promoting-eco-consciousness-a-fusion-of-natural.jpg "Sustainable Sculpture Balance Promoting Eco Consciousness a Fusion of Natural Elements for Green Lifestyle")

## Intermediate

Stepping beyond the foundational understanding, we begin to see **Global Ethics Frameworks** as dynamic systems, constantly being shaped by cultural exchange, technological advancements, and evolving societal values. They aren’t static rulebooks, but rather living documents, reflecting ongoing dialogues about shared values and responsibilities in an increasingly complex world. At this level, we appreciate that applying [ethical principles Meaning → Guiding moral values that shape choices for individual well-being, societal health, and ecological balance.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-principles/)  globally isn’t always straightforward; it involves grappling with diverse perspectives and sometimes conflicting priorities.

![Under a dusky sky a dramatic volcanic crater vividly displays earth’s raw power. Intense geothermal energy glows from within, a natural resource highlighting the planet's immense geological forces. This stark landscape showcases the visible ecological impact of volcanic activity, a potent reminder of earth’s dynamic natural processes.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/dramatic-volcanic-crater-at-dusk-displaying-earths-raw-geothermal-power-a.jpg "Dramatic Volcanic Crater at Dusk Displaying Earth’s Raw Geothermal Power a Natural Sustainable Resource")

### Cultural Lenses and Ethical Variations

One significant dimension to acknowledge is the influence of culture. What is considered ethically sound can vary across different societies and cultural backgrounds. Concepts of individual responsibility versus collective responsibility, for instance, are viewed differently in various parts of the world. Western ethical traditions might emphasize individual rights and autonomy, while Eastern philosophies might prioritize harmony and community well-being.

These differences aren’t necessarily contradictions, but rather diverse ways of approaching ethical dilemmas. A deeper appreciation of Global [Ethics Frameworks involves Meaning → Ethical guidelines for our relationship with the environment, guiding sustainable and responsible living.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/environmental-ethics-frameworks/)  recognizing these cultural lenses and seeking common ground rather than imposing a single ethical viewpoint.

Consider these examples of cultural nuances:

Navigating these cultural variations requires intercultural competence – the ability to understand and interact effectively with people from different cultures. It demands empathy, open-mindedness, and a willingness to learn from diverse perspectives. Global Ethics Frameworks, therefore, are not about imposing a uniform ethical code, but about facilitating dialogue and collaboration across cultures to address shared challenges in ethically sound ways.

![Soft flowing earth tones and organic shapes in this abstract artwork evoke a mindful and sustainable lifestyle. Minimalist art design principles represent resource conservation and an eco-friendly approach. The natural aesthetic inspires sustainable living practices and design values, promoting a calming atmosphere and a conscious path toward a greener more sustainable life within an environmentally aware lifestyle.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-flowing-natural-organic-earth-tones-representing-sustainable-design.jpg "Abstract Flowing Natural Organic Earth Tones Representing Sustainable Design and Eco Conscious Lifestyle Principles")

### Ethical Dilemmas in a Globalized World

As interconnectedness deepens, so do the [ethical dilemmas Meaning → Navigating complex choices where values and sustainability collide, demanding thoughtful decision-making for a balanced life.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-dilemmas/)  we face. Globalization, while offering immense opportunities, also presents intricate challenges that demand careful ethical consideration. These challenges often involve balancing competing values and navigating complex systems.

Consider the following areas where ethical dilemmas frequently arise:

| Area | Ethical Dilemma | Framework Considerations |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Global Supply Chains** | Ensuring fair labor practices and environmental responsibility in complex, multi-layered production networks spanning multiple countries with varying regulations. | Frameworks must address issues of transparency, accountability, and corporate responsibility across borders. |
| **Technological Advancement** | Balancing the benefits of technologies like artificial intelligence and biotechnology with potential risks to privacy, autonomy, and social equity on a global scale. | Frameworks need to anticipate and address ethical implications of emerging technologies, promoting responsible innovation. |
| **Resource Distribution** | Addressing inequalities in access to essential resources like food, water, and energy, particularly in the context of climate change and population growth. | Frameworks must promote equitable resource allocation and sustainable consumption patterns globally. |

Addressing these dilemmas requires moving beyond simplistic solutions. It necessitates a systemic approach that considers the interconnectedness of global challenges. Global Ethics Frameworks provide a lens for analyzing these complexities, prompting us to ask critical questions → Who benefits? Who is harmed?

What are the long-term consequences? What are our shared responsibilities? Engaging with these frameworks at an intermediate level means wrestling with these difficult questions and developing a more sophisticated ethical sensibility.

Furthermore, it is essential to acknowledge that ethical frameworks themselves are not immune to critique. Some argue that existing frameworks are too Western-centric, failing to adequately represent diverse cultural perspectives. Others criticize their lack of enforcement mechanisms or their vulnerability to being co-opted by powerful interests. A mature engagement with Global Ethics Frameworks involves critical reflection, acknowledging their limitations, and actively participating in their ongoing development and refinement.

![Zen stones balance with wood and pink salt demonstrating sustainable living with nature. The image invokes a calm zen vibe epitomizing environmentally conscious principles. This eco-responsibility inspires green ethics with thoughtful earth friendly resources creating a harmonious earth stewardship.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-balance-zen-stones-with-natural-elements-a-green-lifestyle.jpg "Sustainable Balance Zen Stones with Natural Elements a Green Lifestyle Statement of Eco Consciousness")

## Academic

From an academic standpoint, **Global Ethics Frameworks** transcend simple guidelines, representing sophisticated intellectual constructs designed to grapple with the moral complexities of an interconnected world. They are born from interdisciplinary scholarship, drawing upon philosophy, political science, sociology, anthropology, and environmental studies, among others. These frameworks seek to establish a coherent and justifiable basis for ethical action on a planetary scale, acknowledging the inherent tensions between universal principles and diverse local contexts. The academic study of Global Ethics Frameworks involves rigorous analysis of their philosophical underpinnings, their practical applications, and their limitations in addressing real-world challenges.

![This minimalist home office embodies sustainable living through its eco conscious design, emphasizing natural light and durable materials. The workspace promotes a clutter free and calm environment with its neutral tones and functional furniture, fostering mindful work habits and responsible choices. The design reflects resource conservation and ethical consumption principles, key to a low impact and sustainable lifestyle within a home setting, showcasing an expert like approach to green living.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-home-office-interior-design-emphasizing-natural-light-and.jpg "Sustainable Home Office Interior Design Emphasizing Natural Light and Minimalist Aesthetic for Eco Conscious Living")

### Redefining Global Ethics → A Critical Lens

Traditional approaches to ethics often focused on nation-states or specific communities. [Global Ethics Frameworks represent Diversity ensures algorithms serve all communities equitably, mitigating bias and promoting fairness in automated decisions.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/question/why-should-algorithm-training-datasets-represent-community-diversity/)  a paradigm shift, acknowledging the need for ethical considerations that extend beyond geopolitical boundaries. However, a critical academic analysis reveals that defining and implementing such frameworks is fraught with challenges. One central tension lies in the very concept of universality.

Can ethical principles truly be universal, applicable to all cultures and contexts, or are they inevitably shaped by particular cultural and historical perspectives? This debate forms a cornerstone of academic discourse on global ethics.

Exploring diverse perspectives, we might analyze:

From an academic viewpoint, there is no single, universally accepted definition of Global Ethics Frameworks. Instead, the field is characterized by ongoing debate and contestation. This dynamism is not a weakness, but rather a sign of intellectual vitality. The critical examination of different perspectives and the continuous refinement of ethical concepts are essential for developing frameworks that are both theoretically robust and practically relevant.

![Elegant beige coat on a mannequin showcases sustainable fashion with a minimalist and timeless design. Its classic collar and neutral beige tone promotes a versatile and eco-conscious wardrobe. Two understated brown buttons subtly fasten the garment at the neckline emphasizing quality and ethical style choices for a responsible and sustainable lifestyle.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/elegant-beige-sustainable-coat-on-mannequin-showcases-timeless-minimalist.jpg "Elegant Beige Sustainable Coat on Mannequin Showcases Timeless Minimalist Design and Eco Conscious Fashion for Ethical Choices in a Sustainable Lifestyle and Wardrobe")

### Cross-Sectoral Influences and Impact → The Case of Economic Globalization

To understand the real-world impact of Global Ethics Frameworks, consider the domain of economic globalization. The expansion of global trade and financial flows has created unprecedented wealth and interconnectedness, but it has also raised profound ethical questions about inequality, exploitation, and environmental degradation. Analyzing economic globalization through the lens of Global Ethics Frameworks reveals the complex interplay of economic, social, and ethical dimensions.

Focusing on the impact of economic globalization, we can observe:

Applying Global Ethics Frameworks to economic globalization is not simply about imposing moral constraints on economic activity. Rather, it is about re-envisioning economic systems to align with broader ethical values. This might involve promoting fair trade practices, advocating for stronger environmental regulations, supporting social safety nets, and fostering a more inclusive and equitable global economy. From an academic perspective, the challenge lies in developing robust theoretical models and practical strategies that can effectively integrate [ethical considerations Meaning → Ethical considerations are the principles guiding our choices to ensure fairness, justice, and well-being for all, now and in the future.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-considerations/)  into the complex dynamics of global economic systems.

The long-term consequences of ignoring ethical dimensions in economic globalization are potentially severe, ranging from increased social unrest and political instability to environmental catastrophes and the erosion of social cohesion. Academic inquiry into Global Ethics Frameworks, therefore, is not merely an abstract intellectual exercise, but a vital undertaking with profound implications for the [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  of humanity. It requires ongoing critical reflection, interdisciplinary collaboration, and a commitment to developing ethical frameworks that are both theoretically sound and practically effective in navigating the complexities of our interconnected world. This ongoing process of refinement and application is essential for fostering a more just, sustainable, and flourishing global community.

--- SOURCE 3: Shared Ethical Values: Global Consensus? ---
URL: https://leadingincontext.com/2011/08/31/shared-ethical-values-global-consensus/

SUMMARY:
The Institute for Global Ethics conducted a survey to discover whether or not there are universally shared global values: “The 272 survey

FULL CONTENT:
[Leading in Context](https://leadingincontext.com/)

Unleash the Positive Power of Ethical Leadership

# Shared Ethical Values: Global Consensus?

**![](https://leadingincontext.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/08/3-windows.jpg?w=300&h=225 "3 Windows")Are we Approaching a Consensus About Global Ethics?**

![](https://leadingincontext.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/08/3-windows.jpg?w=300&h=225 "3 Windows")

Rushworth Kidder in [Trust: A Primer on Current Thinking](http://http//www.globalethics.org/resources/Trust-A-Primer-on-Current-Thinking/15/)says that “the work of the Institute for Global Ethics suggests that there are indeed core global values that transcend individual cultures.”

As we struggle day-to-day with what ethics means in business, groups of concerned leaders around the world are studying common ethical values that could clarify ethical behavior and unite us in a common global code of ethics.

**Global Values Transcend Boundaries**

The Institute for Global Ethics conducted a survey to discover whether or not there are universally shared global values:

“The 272 survey respondents–representing 40 countries and more than 50 faith communities–identified a core of values centering strongly on truth, compassion, and responsibility. This core appears to be largely unaffected by the respondents’ gender, nationality, native language, or religious affiliation.”

[Global Values, Moral Boundaries: A Pilot Survey](http://www.globalethics.org/resources/Global-Values-Moral-Boundaries-A-Pilot-Survey/273/) (download requires registering) The Institute for Global Ethics, globalethics.org

**A Global Understanding of  Business Ethics**

There are two resources readily available that present ethical values in a global context and provide guidance for ethical corporate behavior.  The Caux Roundtable **Principles for Responsible Business** and **Principles for Responsible Globalization** provide benchmarks for ethical corporate behavior and are available free online. Responsible businesses are reviewing them and discussing ways to abide by the principles.

 “The CRT Principles for Business are a worldwide vision for ethical and responsible corporate behavior and serve as a foundation for action for business leaders worldwide. As a statement of aspirations, the CRT Principles aim to express a world standard against which business behavior can be measured.” [Caux Round Table Principles for Business](http://www.cauxroundtable.org/index.cfm?&menuid=8) at cauxroundtable.org

The CRT Principles of Globalization include new principles for Governments, in addition to the Principles for Business. Just as the Principles for Business, these Principles of Government derive from two ethical ideals: “Kyosei” and “Human Dignity.” The Japanese concept of “Kyosei” looks to living and working together for the common good, while the moral vision of “Human Dignity” refers to the sacredness or value of each person as an end, not simply as a means to the fulfillment of others’ purposes or even of majority demands.”   [Principles for Responsible Globalization](http://www.cauxroundtable.org/index.cfm?&menuid=103) at cauxroundtable.org

enriched_points.expanded_content: Shared values like truthfulness, fairness, and compassion are essential for fostering global ethics, creating a foundation for mutual respect across diverse cultures.
Religious leaders can bridge cultural divides, promoting ethical dialogue and understanding, as seen in the collaborative initiatives addressing global challenges.
Collaborative efforts among different faiths and cultures can yield innovative solutions to pressing global issues, as illustrated by shared ethical frameworks and participatory decision-making.
